name: Environmental Impact Assessment Requirements
description: Multi-jurisdictional Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) requirements covering the EU EIA Directive and GCC regulatory frameworks.

stages.name: Screening
stages.description: Determine whether a proposed project requires an EIA based on project type, size, location, and potential environmental significance.
outputs: Project categorisation (Category A/B/C or Annex I/II)
keyConsiderations: Location sensitivity (proximity to protected areas, water bodies, population centres)
Cumulative effects with other existing or planned projects

stages.name: Scoping
stages.description: Determine the scope and level of detail of the EIA, identify key issues and impacts to assess, define the study area, and establish the assessment methodology.
outputs: Terms of Reference for the EIA study
keyConsiderations: Identification of valued environmental and social components
Spatial and temporal boundaries of the assessment
Assessment methodologies for each impact category

stages.name: Impact Assessment
stages.description: Systematic identification, prediction, and evaluation of potential environmental and social impacts of the proposed project, including baseline studies, impact prediction, and significance evaluation.
keyConsiderations: Impact prediction using modelling, expert judgement, or analogy
Mitigation measures following the mitigation hierarchy

stages.name: Review
stages.description: Independent review of the EIS by the competent authority and public/stakeholder review to ensure adequacy, accuracy, and completeness.
keyConsiderations: Quality of the Environmental Management Plan

stages.name: Decision-making
stages.description: The competent authority makes a decision on whether to approve the project, with or without conditions, based on the EIS, review findings, and public consultation.
keyConsiderations: Compliance with environmental standards and policies

stages.name: Post-decision Monitoring
stages.description: Implementation of the Environmental Management Plan, monitoring of actual impacts, and auditing of compliance with approval conditions.

eu.directiveName: Directive 2014/52/EU amending Directive 2011/92/EU on the assessment of the effects of certain public and private projects on the environment
eu.competentAuthorities: Designated by each EU Member State

annexI.description: Projects requiring mandatory EIA in all cases
examples: Crude oil refineries
Thermal power stations >= 300 MW
Nuclear power stations and reactors
Integrated steel smelting installations
Installations for asbestos extraction/processing
Integrated chemical installations
Motorways and express roads
Railways for long-distance traffic
Airports with runway >= 2100m
Inland waterways and ports for >= 1350 tonne vessels
Waste disposal installations for incineration/treatment of hazardous waste
Groundwater abstraction or recharge >= 10 million m3/year
Dams/water storage >= 10 million m3
Pipelines for gas, oil, chemicals >= 800mm diameter and >= 40 km
Industrial installations for pulp from timber
Large-scale primary extraction of minerals (quarrying, open-cast mining, peat extraction)

annexII.description: Projects requiring screening to determine if EIA is needed based on case-by-case examination or thresholds set by Member States
examples: Agriculture (land consolidation, water management, afforestation, intensive livestock)
Extractive industry (quarries, open-cast mining, underground mining, surface drilling)
Energy industry (industrial installations for electricity/steam/hot water, pipelines, surface storage of natural gas, underground storage of combustible gases)
Metalworking and glass industry
Chemical industry (not covered by Annex I)
Food industry
Textile, leather, wood, and paper industries
Infrastructure projects (industrial estate development, urban development, ski-runs, marinas)
Tourism and leisure (holiday villages, hotel complexes outside urban areas, theme parks)

requiredContent: Description of the project (location, design, size, characteristics during construction and operation)
Description of reasonable alternatives studied and reasons for the chosen option
Description of the relevant aspects of the current state of the environment (baseline)
Description of factors likely to be significantly affected: population, human health, biodiversity, land, soil, water, air, climate, material assets, cultural heritage, landscape
Description of likely significant effects on the environment resulting from construction, existence, use, and where relevant, demolition
Description of forecasting methods and evidence used to identify and assess significant effects
Description of measures envisaged to avoid, prevent, reduce, or offset significant adverse effects
Description of expected significant adverse effects resulting from the vulnerability of the project to risks of major accidents and/or disasters
Reference list of studies and sources used
A monitoring programme for significant adverse effects

saudi.previousAuthority: General Authority of Meteorology and Environmental Protection (GAMEP) / Presidency of Meteorology and Environment (PME)
saudi.legislation: General Environmental Law (Royal Decree No. M/165, 2020); EIA Regulation

projectCategories.categoryA: Major projects with potentially significant impacts requiring full EIA
projectCategories.categoryB: Projects with moderate impacts requiring limited environmental assessment
projectCategories.categoryC: Projects with minimal impacts requiring environmental registration only

requiredContent: Identification and assessment of environmental and social impacts

uae.authority: Federal Authority for Nuclear Regulation (FANR) for nuclear; Ministry of Climate Change and Environment (MoCCaE) + local authorities (EAD Abu Dhabi, EPDA Ras Al Khaimah, etc.)
uae.legislation: Federal Law No. 24 of 1999 (Protection and Development of the Environment); Cabinet Decision No. 37 of 2001 (EIA Regulations); Emirate-level regulations

projectCategories.category1: Projects requiring full Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) or EIA
projectCategories.category2: Projects requiring Environmental Management Plan (EMP) only
projectCategories.category3: Projects exempt from EIA, requiring no-objection certificate (NOC)

keyStandards: Abu Dhabi EHSMS (Environmental, Health & Safety Management System)

qatar.authority: Ministry of Municipality and Environment (MME) / Environmental Protection Department
qatar.legislation: Law No. 30 of 2002 (Environmental Protection Law); EIA guidelines

projectCategories.category1: Major industrial and infrastructure projects requiring full EIA
projectCategories.category2: Medium-scale projects requiring simplified environmental assessment
projectCategories.category3: Small projects requiring environmental permit only

keyStandards: Qatar National Ambient Air Quality Standards
QP HSE Regulations (for oil and gas)

impactCategories.name: Air Quality
impactCategories.description: Impacts on ambient and local air quality from emissions of pollutants including particulate matter, nitrogen oxides, sulphur dioxide, volatile organic compounds, carbon monoxide, and hazardous air pollutants.
assessmentMethodology: Comparison with applicable ambient air quality standards

significanceCriteria.major: Predicted exceedance of ambient air quality standards at sensitive receptor locations
significanceCriteria.moderate: Predicted contribution >25% of applicable standard at any sensitive receptor
significanceCriteria.minor: Predicted contribution >10% but <25% of applicable standard

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: process selection to eliminate emissions sources
Minimisation: pollution control equipment (scrubbers, filters, SCR/SNCR)
Restoration: not typically applicable
Offset: emissions trading or offset programmes

impactCategories.name: Water Resources
impactCategories.description: Impacts on surface water and groundwater quantity and quality, including water abstraction, discharge of effluents, changes to hydrology and drainage patterns.
assessmentMethodology: Hydrological survey and water balance assessment
Water quality modelling for discharge scenarios

significanceCriteria.major: Significant alteration of water body status or exceedance of discharge standards affecting downstream users or ecology
significanceCriteria.moderate: Measurable deterioration in water quality or quantity but within regulatory limits
significanceCriteria.minor: Localised and temporary effects on water quality or quantity
significanceCriteria.negligible: No measurable change in water quality or quantity

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: water-efficient process design, closed-loop cooling systems
Minimisation: wastewater treatment, stormwater management, efficient irrigation
Restoration: riparian zone restoration, wetland enhancement
Offset: water replenishment projects in the same watershed

impactCategories.name: Soil and Land
impactCategories.description: Impacts on soil quality, land use, land stability, and contamination risks from construction and operational activities.
assessmentMethodology: Contaminated land assessment (Phase I/II Environmental Site Assessment)

significanceCriteria.major: Widespread soil contamination above intervention values, loss of prime agricultural land, or significant landslide risk
significanceCriteria.moderate: Localised soil contamination, measurable loss of soil function, or moderate erosion
significanceCriteria.minor: Limited and reversible soil disturbance within the project footprint
significanceCriteria.negligible: No measurable change to soil quality or function

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: site selection to avoid sensitive soils, brownfield preference
Minimisation: erosion control, soil management plans, spill prevention
Restoration: soil remediation, topsoil reinstatement, revegetation
Offset: agricultural land improvement or soil conservation projects

impactCategories.name: Biodiversity
impactCategories.description: Impacts on habitats, flora, fauna, and ecological connectivity, including effects on protected species, critical habitats, and ecosystem services.
assessmentMethodology: Phase 1 habitat survey and extended Phase 2 surveys (species-specific)
Ecological baseline surveys (seasonal, multi-year for large projects)
Population viability analysis for key species

significanceCriteria.major: Loss or fragmentation of critical habitat, significant impact on IUCN CR/EN species population viability, or loss of irreplaceable biodiversity
significanceCriteria.moderate: Measurable loss of natural/modified habitat, impact on VU species or high conservation value areas
significanceCriteria.minor: Localised habitat disturbance with low ecological sensitivity, affecting common species
significanceCriteria.negligible: No measurable impact on habitats or species of conservation concern

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: site and route selection to avoid critical habitats and Key Biodiversity Areas
Minimisation: reduced footprint, construction timing to avoid sensitive periods, wildlife crossings
Restoration: habitat reinstatement, native species planting, ecological connectivity restoration
Offset: biodiversity offsets achieving no net loss or net gain (measurable conservation outcomes)

impactCategories.name: Noise and Vibration
impactCategories.description: Impacts from construction and operational noise and vibration on sensitive receptors including residential areas, hospitals, schools, and wildlife habitats.
assessmentMethodology: Baseline noise monitoring (LAeq, LA90, LAmax at receptor locations)
Noise modelling and prediction (ISO 9613-2, CRTN, CadnaA)
Assessment against applicable noise standards and guidelines

significanceCriteria.major: Exceedance of statutory noise limits at sensitive receptors, or levels causing significant adverse health effects
significanceCriteria.moderate: Noticeable increase (>5 dB) above baseline at sensitive receptors

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: site layout optimisation, setback distances
Minimisation: noise barriers, equipment enclosures, low-noise equipment, restricted working hours
Restoration: not typically applicable
Offset: insulation programmes for affected properties

impactCategories.name: Visual and Landscape
impactCategories.description: Impacts on landscape character, visual amenity, and scenic quality from project infrastructure, lighting, and land use change.
assessmentMethodology: Landscape and Visual Impact Assessment (LVIA) per GLVIA3
Photomontages and visualisations from key viewpoints

significanceCriteria.major: Fundamental change to a landscape of high sensitivity, or dominant visual impact from key viewpoints
significanceCriteria.moderate: Noticeable change to landscape character or visual amenity
significanceCriteria.minor: Slight change, not altering the overall landscape character
significanceCriteria.negligible: No discernible change to landscape or visual amenity

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: site selection and layout to avoid sensitive landscapes and viewpoints
Minimisation: architectural design, colour and material selection, landscape screening, lighting design (dark-sky compliant)
Restoration: landscape reinstatement and planting schemes
Offset: landscape enhancement or conservation in the surrounding area

impactCategories.name: Socioeconomic
impactCategories.description: Impacts on local communities including employment, livelihoods, housing, public services, health, land acquisition, and community cohesion.
assessmentMethodology: Socioeconomic baseline survey (census data, household surveys, key informant interviews)
Land acquisition and resettlement assessment (if applicable)
Health Impact Assessment (HIA) for major projects
Economic impact modelling (input-output analysis, multiplier effects)

significanceCriteria.major: Physical displacement of communities, loss of primary livelihood source, or significant adverse health outcomes
significanceCriteria.moderate: Measurable change in employment, access to services, or community dynamics
significanceCriteria.minor: Localised and temporary disruption to community activities

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: site selection to avoid displacement and livelihood disruption
Minimisation: local hiring, community investment, livelihood restoration programmes
Restoration: resettlement action plans meeting IFC PS5 standards
Offset: community development programmes, benefit-sharing mechanisms

impactCategories.name: Cultural Heritage
impactCategories.description: Impacts on tangible cultural heritage (archaeological sites, historic buildings, monuments) and intangible cultural heritage (traditions, practices, sacred sites).
assessmentMethodology: Desktop study of heritage databases and historical records
Consultation with relevant heritage authorities and local communities

significanceCriteria.major: Destruction or major alteration of nationally/internationally significant heritage assets
significanceCriteria.moderate: Partial damage or setting alteration of significant heritage assets
significanceCriteria.minor: Limited and reversible impact on heritage assets of local significance
significanceCriteria.negligible: No impact on known heritage assets

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: project redesign to avoid heritage assets and their settings
Minimisation: archaeological excavation and recording, protective measures during construction
Restoration: conservation and restoration of affected heritage assets
Offset: support for heritage conservation programmes

impactCategories.name: Traffic and Transport
impactCategories.description: Impacts from construction and operational traffic on road network capacity, road safety, pedestrian and cyclist amenity, and access to properties.

significanceCriteria.major: Significant increase in traffic volumes (>30%) on sensitive routes, or material road safety risk
significanceCriteria.moderate: Noticeable increase in traffic (10-30%) on affected routes
significanceCriteria.minor: Minor increase in traffic (<10%) on affected routes
significanceCriteria.negligible: No discernible change in traffic conditions

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: alternative transport modes (rail, pipeline, conveyor), site access design
Minimisation: construction traffic management plans, route restrictions, timing controls
Restoration: road reinstatement after construction
Offset: road improvement contributions, public transport investment

impactCategories.name: Waste Management
impactCategories.description: Impacts from generation, storage, transport, treatment, and disposal of solid and liquid wastes, including hazardous wastes.
assessmentMethodology: Risk assessment for hazardous waste handling and transport

significanceCriteria.major: Generation of significant hazardous waste quantities without adequate disposal facilities, or risk of uncontrolled waste release
significanceCriteria.moderate: Significant non-hazardous waste generation requiring new disposal capacity
significanceCriteria.minor: Moderate waste generation manageable within existing facilities and practices
significanceCriteria.negligible: Minimal waste generation with established management routes

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: process design to eliminate waste streams, design for deconstruction
Minimisation: waste reduction at source, reuse and recycling programmes
Restoration: remediation of contaminated land from historical waste disposal
Offset: waste-to-energy or beneficial reuse projects

impactCategories.name: Climate
impactCategories.description: Climate-related impacts including project GHG emissions contribution and project vulnerability to climate change effects.
assessmentMethodology: GHG emissions inventory (Scope 1, 2, 3 per GHG Protocol)
Carbon footprint assessment (construction and operational phases)
Alignment with national and sectoral carbon budgets

significanceCriteria.major: Annual emissions >100,000 tCO2e, or project undermines national climate commitments
significanceCriteria.minor: Annual emissions <25,000 tCO2e with mitigation measures in place

mitigationHierarchy: Avoidance: project alternatives with lower carbon intensity
Minimisation: energy efficiency, renewable energy, low-carbon materials, electrification
Restoration: ecosystem restoration for carbon sequestration
Offset: verified carbon offsets for residual emissions
